content:
# Galatians Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/GAL_004_page_001.jpg, GAL_004_page_002.jpg

1. I mean that the heir, as long as he is a child, is no different from a slave, though he is the owner of everything,

2. but he is under guardians and managers until the date set by his father.

3. In the same way we also, when we were children, were enslaved to the elementary principles of the world.

4. But when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of a woman, born under the law,

5. to redeem those who were under the law, so that we might receive adoption as sons.

6. And because you are sons, God has sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying, "Abba! Father!"

7. So you are no longer a slave, but a son, and if a son, then an heir through God.

8. Formerly, when you did not know God, you were enslaved to those that by nature are not gods.

9. But now that you have come to know God, or rather to be known by God, how can you turn back again to the weak and worthless elementary principles of the world, whose slaves you want to be once more?

10. You observe days and months and seasons and years!

11. I am afraid I may have labored over you in vain.

12. Brothers, I entreat you, become as I am, for I also became as you are. You did me no wrong.

13. You know it was because of a bodily ailment that I preached the gospel to you at first,

14. and though my condition was a trial to you, you did not scorn or despise me, but received me as an angel of God, as Christ Jesus.

15. What then has become of your blessedness? For I testify to you that, if possible, you would have gouged out your eyes and given them to me.

16. Have I then become your enemy by telling you the truth?

17. They make much of you, but for no good purpose. They want to shut you out, that you may make much of them.

18. It is always good to be made much of for a good purpose, and not only when I am present with you,

19. my little children, for whom I am again in the anguish of childbirth until Christ is formed in you!

20. I wish I could be present with you now and change my tone, for I am perplexed about you.

21. Tell me, you who desire to be under the law, do you not listen to the law?

22. For it is written that Abraham had two sons, one by a slave woman and one by a free woman.

23. But the son of the slave was born according to the flesh, while the son of the free woman was born through promise.

24. Now this may be interpreted allegorically: these women are two covenants. One is from Mount Sinai, bearing children for slavery; she is Hagar.

25. Now Hagar is Mount Sinai in Arabia; she corresponds to the present Jerusalem, for she is in slavery with her children.

26. But the Jerusalem above is free, and she is our mother.

27. For it is written, "Rejoice, O barren one who does not bear; break forth and cry aloud, you who are not in labor! For the children of the desolate one will be more than those of the one who has a husband."

28. Now you, brothers, like Isaac, are children of promise.

29. But just as at that time he who was born according to the flesh persecuted him who was born according to the Spirit, so also it is now.

30. But what does the Scripture say? "Cast out the slave woman and her son, for the son of the slave woman shall not inherit with the son of the free woman."

31. So, brothers, we are not children of the slave but of the free woman.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 4 moves from theological argument to personal appeal and back to allegory. The stoicheia question (vv.3, 9), the personal plea (vv.12-20), and the Hagar/Sarah allegory (vv.21-31) are the three major sections.

**Verse 4 — "The fullness of time":** All three translators preserved this phrase. SOPHIA affirms: this is one of Paul's most theologically dense expressions — time itself has a structure, and God's action is calibrated to it.

**Verse 6 — "Abba! Father!":** All three translators preserved the Aramaic-Greek doublet. SOPHIA affirms: the retention of the Aramaic in a Greek letter is evidence of the earliest christological prayer tradition.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "I say" vs. "I mean" vs. "Think of it this way"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And I say, for as long a time as the heir is a child" | legō de — "and I say" |
| LOGOS | "I mean that the heir, as long as he is a child" | "I mean" — clarifying function |
| PNEUMA | "Think of it this way. If a father dies and leaves an inheritance for his young children" | Full restructuring; adds "father dies," "leaves an inheritance" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds narrative elements not in the Greek — "if a father dies," "leaves an inheritance," "young children." Paul's analogy is about legal minority, not inheritance upon death.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds narrative content.

---

### v.3 — "elements of the world" vs. "elementary principles of the world" vs. "basic spiritual forces of this world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the elements of the world" | ta stoicheia tou kosmou — literal |
| LOGOS | "the elementary principles of the world" | "Elementary principles" — pedagogical |
| PNEUMA | "the basic spiritual forces of this world" | "Spiritual forces" — cosmic powers reading |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. stoicheia tou kosmou is genuinely ambiguous — "elements," "principles," or "spirits." LOGOS's "elementary principles" preserves the ambiguity between educational and cosmic readings. PNEUMA's "spiritual forces" commits to the cosmic powers interpretation.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. stoicheia tou kosmou — "elementary principles" vs. "spiritual forces." This affects whether Paul sees pre-Christian existence as bondage to rudimentary ideas or to cosmic powers. The ambiguity is probably intentional.

---

### v.4 — "having come from a woman" vs. "born of a woman" vs. "born of a woman"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having come from a woman, having come under law" | genomenon — "having come into being" (not gennēthenta, "having been born") |
| LOGOS | "born of a woman, born under the law" | "Born" — standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "born of a woman, subject to the law" | "Subject to" for "under" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. genomenon (from ginomai, "become") is rendered "born" by convention, though EMET correctly notes it is not the standard word for birth (gennaō). PNEUMA's "subject to the law" changes the preposition — Paul says "under" (hypo) law, not "subject to" it, preserving the bondage metaphor from v.3.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — genomenon vs. gennēthenta; PNEUMA softens "under."

---

### v.6 — "crying" vs. "crying" vs. "prompting us to call out"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "crying, 'Abba! Father!'" | krazon — present active participle |
| LOGOS | "crying, 'Abba! Father!'" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "prompting us to call out, 'Abba, Father'" | "Prompting us to call out" — mediated action |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says the Spirit itself cries "Abba!" — the Spirit is the agent, not a prompter. PNEUMA changes direct action ("crying") to mediated action ("prompting us to call out"), which subtly shifts the theology of prayer.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Spirit as direct agent vs. prompter of human action.

---

### v.8 — "the ones by nature not being gods" vs. "those that by nature are not gods" vs. "so-called gods that do not even exist"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] by nature not being gods" | tois physei mē ousin theois — dative |
| LOGOS | "those that by nature are not gods" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "so-called gods that do not even exist" | "Do not even exist" — ontological denial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says these beings are "not gods by nature" — which leaves open whether they exist as something other than gods. PNEUMA's "do not even exist" makes an ontological claim Paul does not make here. Paul may acknowledge their existence as demonic powers while denying their divinity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA makes ontological claim Paul avoids.

---

### v.9 — "poor elements" vs. "worthless elementary principles" vs. "useless spiritual powers"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the weak and poor elements" | asthene kai ptōcha stoicheia — "weak and beggarly" |
| LOGOS | "the weak and worthless elementary principles" | "Worthless" for ptōcha |
| PNEUMA | "the weak and useless spiritual powers of this world" | "Useless spiritual powers" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. ptōcha literally means "beggarly/poor" — LOGOS's "worthless" captures the contempt. PNEUMA again commits to the "spiritual powers" reading of stoicheia and adds "of this world" (not in this verse's Greek).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Same stoicheia issue as v.3.

---

### v.13 — "weakness of the flesh" vs. "bodily ailment" vs. "I was sick"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because of a weakness of the flesh" | di' astheneian tēs sarkos — literal |
| LOGOS | "because of a bodily ailment" | "Bodily ailment" — contextual rendering |
| PNEUMA | "I was sick" | Simplified |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. astheneian tēs sarkos means "weakness of the flesh" — in context, a physical condition. LOGOS's "bodily ailment" is appropriate. PNEUMA simplifies to "I was sick," which loses the Pauline vocabulary of "flesh" and "weakness" that carries theological weight throughout the letter.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses theologically loaded terminology.

---

### v.14 — "you did not despise nor spit at" vs. "you did not scorn or despise" vs. "you did not despise me or turn me away"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you did not despise nor spit at" | exouthenesate...exeptysate — "despise...spit out" |
| LOGOS | "you did not scorn or despise me" | "Scorn" for exptyō |
| PNEUMA | "you did not despise me or turn me away" | "Turn me away" for exptyō |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. ekptyō literally means "spit out" — an apotropaic gesture against illness or evil. LOGOS's "scorn" captures the contempt without the literal image. PNEUMA's "turn me away" loses the physical disgust Paul evokes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. ekptyō — "spit at" vs. "scorn" vs. "turn away."

---

### v.17 — "zealous" vs. "make much of" vs. "eager to win your favor"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "They are zealous for you, not rightly...that you might be zealous for them" | zēlousin — "are zealous for" |
| LOGOS | "They make much of you...that you may make much of them" | "Make much of" — captures the courting |
| PNEUMA | "Those false teachers are so eager to win your favor...so that you will pay attention only to them" | "False teachers" added; "eager to win your favor" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. zēloō means "be zealous for / court eagerly." LOGOS's "make much of" captures the social dynamic. PNEUMA adds "false teachers" (not in Greek — Paul says simply "they") and expands "zealous" into "eager to win your favor."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA identifies unspecified "they" as "false teachers."

---

### v.19 — "my children" vs. "my little children" vs. "Oh, my dear children"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "My children, for whom again I am in labor pains" | tekna mou — "my children" |
| LOGOS | "my little children, for whom I am again in the anguish of childbirth" | teknia — "little children" (diminutive) |
| PNEUMA | "Oh, my dear children! I feel as if I'm going through labor pains for you again" | "Oh"; "dear"; "I feel as if" — softens |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Sinaiticus reading is tekna ("children"), though some manuscripts have teknia ("little children"). LOGOS's "little children" follows a variant reading. PNEUMA adds "Oh," "dear," and "I feel as if" — softening the directness of Paul's metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds emotive language.

---

### v.23 — "born according to the flesh" vs. "born according to the flesh" vs. "born in a human attempt to bring about the fulfillment of God's promise"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "has been born according to [the] flesh" | kata sarka gegennētai |
| LOGOS | "was born according to the flesh" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "was born in a human attempt to bring about the fulfillment of God's promise" | Full expansion of "according to the flesh" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kata sarka ("according to the flesh") means "by natural/human means." PNEUMA expands this into a full interpretive clause — "in a human attempt to bring about the fulfillment of God's promise" — which is a particular theological reading, not a translation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds extensive interpretation.

---

### v.25 — "the present Hierousalēm/Jerusalem"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "corresponds to the present Hierousalēm" | systoichei — "stands in the same column/corresponds" |
| LOGOS | "she corresponds to the present Jerusalem" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "And now Jerusalem is just like Mount Sinai in Arabia" | "Just like" — colloquial; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. systoicheō means "stand in the same column/correspond to" — a technical term from columnar arrangement. PNEUMA's "just like" loses the technical precision and restructures the sentence.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. systoicheō — technical correspondence vs. simile.

---

### v.29 — "according to the Spirit" vs. "according to the Spirit" vs. "by the power of the Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] [born] according to [the] Spirit" | kata pneuma |
| LOGOS | "him who was born according to the Spirit" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Isaac, the child born by the power of the Spirit" | "By the power of" — adds "power"; names Isaac |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kata pneuma means "according to the Spirit" — PNEUMA adds "the power of," which is not in the text. Paul says simply "according to Spirit," paralleling "according to flesh."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "power."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 7 | "False teachers," "human attempt," "prompting us," "do not exist," "spiritual forces/powers" |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 3 | Restructures allegory (v.25), softens metaphor (v.19), adds narrative (v.1) |
| stoicheia ambiguity | 2 | "Elementary principles" vs. "spiritual forces" (vv.3, 9) |
| EMET transliteration | 1 | Hierousalēm |
| Theological vocabulary | 2 | "Weakness of flesh" vs. "sick" (v.13); Spirit as agent vs. prompter (v.6) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 4's most significant disagreement is the rendering of stoicheia tou kosmou (vv.3, 9) — whether Paul describes bondage to "elementary principles" (educational metaphor) or "spiritual forces" (cosmic powers). PNEUMA consistently commits to the cosmic powers reading, which has scholarly support but forecloses the educational reading that fits the guardian/child metaphor of vv.1-2. The personal section (vv.12-20) shows PNEUMA softening Paul's raw emotional language, and the allegory section shows PNEUMA expanding compressed theological phrases into full interpretive clauses.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
